_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Toxins and Environmental: Beta Blocker Poisoning/Overdose. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Beta Blocker Poisoning/Overdose

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Beta Blocker Poisoning/Overdose
points: Reduce GI absorption of oral agents with some form of binding agent (activated charcoal) especially for extended release
Early airway protection is required as patients may have rapid mental status deterioration
Assure adequate ventilation, oxygenation, and correction of hypoperfusion

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patients may present with: a. Bradycardia
Beta blocker agent examples: a. Acebutolol hydrochloride (Sectral')
Alpha/beta-adrenergic blocking agents' examples: a. Carvedilol (Coreg')
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
None noted

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety; activate ALS
Oxygen via NRM; BVM for respiratory depression
CPR/AED for cardiac arrest
Supine position; protect airway

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: 12-lead ECG: bradycardia, hypotension, widened QRS common
IV access maintenance
Oral activated charcoal if: conscious, alert, recent ingestion (<2 hrs), intact gag reflex — only per medical direction order

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation; IV NS bolus 500-1000 mL for hypotension
Atropine 0.5-1 mg IV for symptomatic bradycardia (often ineffective in BB OD)
Glucagon 1-5 mg IV/IO bolus (bypasses beta receptor) for bradycardia/hypotension
Waveform capnography; blood glucose monitoring

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: Glucagon 3-5 mg IV bolus, then 2-5 mg/hour infusion for bradycardia/hypotension
High-dose insulin euglycemia (HDIE): regular insulin 1 unit/kg IV bolus + dextrose; maintain euglycemia
Lipid emulsion therapy (intralipid 20%) 1.5 mL/kg IV bolus for cardiac toxicity (per medical direction)
Calcium chloride 1 g IV for CCB co-ingestion
Transcutaneous pacing for refractory bradycardia (may not capture at normal outputs — increase mA)
Vasopressors: norepinephrine or epinephrine infusion for refractory hypotension
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV if QRS >120 ms
RSI and intubation for airway compromise: avoid succinylcholine (hyperkalemia risk); use rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Transcutaneous pacing may not always capture nor correct hypotension when capture is successful
Aspiration of activated charcoal can cause airway management to be nearly impossible. Do not administer activated charcoal to any patients that may have a worsening mental status

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Pediatric Considerations a. Pediatric patient may develop hypoglycemia from beta blocker overdose therefore it is important to perform glucose evaluation
Glucagon has a side effect of increased vomiting at these doses and ondansetron prophylaxis may be considered

Atropine may have little or no effect (likely to be more helpful in mild overdoses) ' the hypotension and bradycardia may be mutually exclusive, and the blood pressure may not respond to correction of bradycardia
Propranolol crosses the blood brain barrier and can cause altered mental status, seizure, and widened QRS similar to TCA toxicity
Certain beta-blockers, such as acebutolol and propranolol, may increase QRS duration
Certain beta-blockers, such as acebutolol and pindolol, may produce tachycardia and hypertension
Sotalol can produce increase in QTc interval and ventricular dysrhythmias
Frequent reassessment is essential as patient deterioration can be rapid and catastrophic

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Repeat evaluation and documentation of signs and symptoms and vital signs as patient clinical conditions may deteriorate rapidly
' Identification of possible etiology of poisoning
' Time of symptoms onset and time of initiation of exposure-specific treatment
' Therapy and response to therapy

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
